stem: A customer analytics dataset contains 150 observations of purchase amount. The data is believed to come from a mixture of two populations: casual buyers (mean ≈ 27.4 $, SD ≈ 5.3) and power users (mean ≈ 45.4 $, SD ≈ 7.9). The proportion of casual buyers in the sample is uncertain, estimated between 50% and 66%. The observed overall mean is 35.0 $ with SD = 11.0. Estimate the true population mean of purchase amount, accounting for the uncertainty in the mixing proportion.

ground_truth.answer: Mixture mean = w×27.4 + (1-w)×45.4. As w varies from 0.5 to 0.66, the mixture mean ranges from 33.5 to 36.4. With sampling variability, CI = [31.7, 38.2]. Point estimate at w=0.58: 35.0 $.

solution_steps: Mixture mean formula: μ_mix = w × μ_A + (1-w) × μ_B.
At w=0.5: μ_mix = 36.4 (more of power users).
At w=0.66: μ_mix = 33.5 (more of casual buyers).
Combined interval [31.7, 38.2] accounts for both mixing uncertainty and sampling variability.
common_failure_modes: Using the simple average of the two component means without weighting
Treating the mixing proportion as known instead of uncertain
Ignoring that the overall SD (11.0) is inflated by the between-component variation
